description: Indicates the current size of the transaction log attached to this database.
interpretation: This reflects the total space currently reserved for the transaction log on the disk.
troubleshootingSteps: Monitor this value to ensure the allocated size aligns with the disk capacity.
If the size is unexpectedly high, check for long-running transactions that prevent log reuse.

description: Indicates the transaction log space that is currently in use.
interpretation: Ideally, this value should be low. High usage indicates that many transactions are being recorded but not yet truncated.
troubleshootingSteps: Check if log backups are running correctly (for Full Recovery model).
Identify high-volume DML operations that might be filling the log.

description: Indicates the transaction log space that is currently free.
interpretation: Ideally, this value should be high so that there is enough space for future transactions.
troubleshootingSteps: If free space is low, consider expanding the log file or performing a log backup/truncation.

description: Indicates the percentage of transaction log space that is currently in use.
interpretation: Ideally, this value should be low. A value close to 100% requires immediate attention as it indicates severe space constraints, risking unrecorded database modifications.
troubleshootingSteps: Analyze the root cause of excessive space usage (e.g., too many changes written to log but not to database).
Perform emergency log truncation if necessary.

description: Indicates the percentage of transaction log space that is currently free.
interpretation: Ideally, this value should be high to ensure headroom for spikes in transaction activity.
troubleshootingSteps: Investigate if the percentage drops consistently during specific scheduled jobs.

description: Indicates whether/not auto-growth is enabled for the transaction logs.
interpretation: Enabling autogrowth allows log files to expand automatically. In graphs, 'Yes' is represented by 100 and 'No' by 0.
troubleshootingSteps: Ensure autogrowth is enabled for mission-critical databases to prevent 'Log Full' errors.
Verify the SQL Server version is 2008 R2 or above.

description: Indicates the percentage by which the transaction log files grow automatically.
interpretation: Growth increment must be large enough to stay ahead of transaction needs but not so large that it causes timeouts or blocking.
troubleshootingSteps: If growth is too small, frequent expansions can cause VLF (Virtual Log File) fragmentation.
If growth is too large, it may cause query timeouts during the expansion process.
Consider setting autogrow in MB instead of Percent for better performance consistency.

description: Indicates the size (in MB) by which the transaction log files grow automatically.
interpretation: Setting growth in MB is generally recommended over percentages. A rule of thumb is to set it to about one-eighth of the file size.
troubleshootingSteps: Check if the growth size matches the recommended one-eighth ratio for optimal testing.

description: Indicates the maximum size to which the transaction log can grow.
interpretation: The MAXSIZE setting prevents a single log file from consuming all available disk space.
troubleshootingSteps: Configure a reasonable maximum size to protect the host OS and other databases on the same drive.

description: Indicates the percentage of maximum log file size that is currently being used.
interpretation: Values close to 100% indicate the log is reaching its configured limit. At 100%, Error 9002 is raised and database changes will fail.
troubleshootingSteps: For Simple Recovery: Ensure transaction log is cleared on all checkpoints; check for long-running transactions.
For Full Recovery: Verify that log backups are successful and frequent enough.
Check if the MAXSIZE or disk space needs to be increased.
